subtopic: Glacier Preservation and Climate Tipping Points
title: Glacier Conservation Initiatives (Global and India)
focus: UN and Indian initiatives for glacier preservation and Himalayan ecosystem research.
rationale: Distinct topic covering international and national efforts for glacier conservation, highly testable for prelims regarding specific years, locations, and projects like ICE-CRUNCH.
sectionExcerpt: 2025 designated as the International Year of Glaciers' Preservation; Decade of Action for Cryospheric Sciences (2025–2034)... ICE-CRUNCH: A collaborative study between scientists of India and Switzerland. India’s first High-Altitude Climate Research Station: In Nathatop, Jammu and Kashmir for Climate research... affiliated with the World Meteorological Organization’s (WMO) Global Atmospheric Watch (GAW) Programme.
evidenceBullets: 2025 is designated as the International Year of Glaciers' Preservation.
The Decade of Action for Cryospheric Sciences spans from 2025 to 2034.
ICE-CRUNCH is an Indo-Swiss collaborative study on ice nucleating particles in the North-Western Himalayas.
India's first High-Altitude Climate Research Station is located in Nathatop, Jammu and Kashmir, and is affiliated with the WMO's Global Atmospheric Watch Programme.

subtopic: Glacier Preservation and Climate Tipping Points
title: India's Polar Research Stations and Maitri II
focus: India's upcoming and existing research bases in Antarctica and the Arctic.
rationale: Standalone topic on India's scientific presence in polar regions, frequently asked in UPSC.
sectionExcerpt: Union Finance Ministry granted approval to Maitri II, new research station in Eastern Antarctica. This will be India’s 4th research base, expected to be operational by January 2029. It would be established as a green research base powered using renewable energy sources... India’s other Polar research stations: Maitri (Antarctica), Bharati (Antarctica), Dakshin Gangotri (first scientific base station of India situated in Antarctica now decommissioned), and Himadri (Arctic).
evidenceBullets: Maitri II is India's upcoming 4th research base in Eastern Antarctica, expected to be operational by 2029.
Maitri II will be a green research base powered by solar and wind energy.
India's Antarctic stations include Dakshin Gangotri (decommissioned), Maitri, and Bharati.
Himadri is India's active research station in the Arctic.
The Indian Antarctic Act was enacted in 2022 to provide a legal framework for India's polar activities.

subtopic: Glacier Preservation and Climate Tipping Points
title: Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) and Net-Zero Banking Alliance
focus: International frameworks for corporate climate disclosures and the banking sector's net-zero transition.
rationale: Groups two related financial and corporate climate initiatives that are important for environment and economy intersections.
sectionExcerpt: The Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) launched the Integrity Matters Checklist, a UN-endorsed framework designed to align corporate climate disclosures with the United Nations’ net-zero standards. Established: 1997... Net-Zero Banking Alliance: It has been dissolved after several member banks withdrew. Established in 2021 under UN Environment Programme's Finance Initiative.
evidenceBullets: GRI launched the Integrity Matters Checklist to align corporate climate disclosures with UN net-zero standards.
GRI was established in 1997 as a non-profit body setting sustainability reporting frameworks.
The Net-Zero Banking Alliance was established in 2021 under the UNEP Finance Initiative but has recently been dissolved.
The Net-Zero Banking Alliance aimed to encourage banks to reduce the carbon footprint of their loans and investments.

subtopic: Glacier Preservation and Climate Tipping Points
title: UAE Consensus and COP28 Outcomes
focus: Key targets and agreements adopted under the UAE Consensus at COP28.
rationale: Major international climate agreement outcome, distinct from other news items and highly relevant for global climate governance questions.
sectionExcerpt: UAE Consensus: A recent UN report highlights the UAE Consensus as a key milestone advancing global clean energy transition. Adopted at COP28 (2023), marked the first-ever Global Stocktake under the Paris Agreement. Urges nations to transition away from fossil fuels in a just, fair, and balanced way. Sets goals to triple renewable energy and double energy efficiency by 2030. Reinforces the collective aim to keep 1.5°C within reach and achieve net zero emissions by 2050.
evidenceBullets: The UAE Consensus was adopted at COP28 in 2023, marking the first-ever Global Stocktake under the Paris Agreement.
It sets global goals to triple renewable energy and double energy efficiency by 2030.
It urges nations to transition away from fossil fuels to achieve net zero emissions by 2050.
The consensus reinforces the collective aim to keep global warming within 1.5 degrees Celsius.

subtopic: Glacier Preservation and Climate Tipping Points
title: Climate Monitoring Projects: AmazonFACE and NASA OCO
focus: Scientific projects monitoring CO2 absorption, forest resilience, and atmospheric carbon.
rationale: Groups two specific scientific monitoring initiatives related to carbon sinks and atmospheric CO2, testing knowledge of environmental monitoring tools.
sectionExcerpt: AmazonFACE: Scientists in Brazil have launched the AmazonFACE (Free-Air CO2 Enrichment) project near Manaus. It seeks to simulate the atmosphere of the future and assess how tropical forests adapt to rising CO2... NASA’ Orbiting Carbon Observatory (OCO): The USA administration plans to shut down NASA’s OCO missions... Key Findings: Amazon rainforest emits more CO2 than it absorbs. Boreal forests in Canada, Russia, and melting permafrost areas absorb more CO2 than they emit.
evidenceBullets: AmazonFACE is a Brazilian project simulating future atmospheres to assess how tropical forests adapt to rising CO2 and temperatures.
NASA's Orbiting Carbon Observatory (OCO) missions are key data sources for tracking atmospheric carbon.
OCO findings reveal that the Amazon rainforest currently emits more CO2 than it absorbs.
Boreal forests and melting permafrost areas in Canada and Russia currently absorb more CO2 than they emit.

subtopic: Glacier Preservation and Climate Tipping Points
title: Climate Tipping Points
focus: Definition, mechanisms, and examples of climate tipping points as identified by the IPCC.
rationale: Core geographical and environmental concept, clearly separated under its own heading, essential for understanding irreversible climate changes.
sectionExcerpt: About climate tipping point: These are critical thresholds in a system that, when exceeded, can lead to a significant change in the state of the system, often with an understanding that the change is irreversible... Tipping points are interlinked, meaning crossing one may accelerate collapse of others... Tipping points identified by IPCC: Amazon rainforest dieback, permafrost collapse, Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) slowdown, Boreal Forest shift, Greenland and Antarctic ice sheets collapse risks.
evidenceBullets: Climate tipping points are critical thresholds that lead to significant, often irreversible, system changes when exceeded.
Tipping points are interlinked, meaning crossing one can create cascading effects that threaten planetary stability.
IPCC-identified tipping points include Amazon dieback, permafrost collapse, AMOC slowdown, and ice sheet collapse.
Warm-water coral reefs have recently crossed their thermal tipping point, triggering irreversible dieback.

subtopic: Glacier Preservation and Climate Tipping Points
title: Arctic Amplification
focus: Causes and impacts of the Arctic warming faster than the rest of the globe.
rationale: Core geographical concept with specific mechanisms (albedo, lapse rate, water vapour) and direct impacts on the Indian monsoon.
sectionExcerpt: About Arctic Amplification: A phenomenon where climate in Arctic warms more quickly than the rest of the Earth. Factors for Arctic Amplification: Reduced Albedo due to melting of reflective ice... Lapse rate feedback (warming from GHG most pronounced near the surface...); Water Vapour Triple Effect... Atmospheric Heat transport from tropics. Impacts on India: Disruption of Indian Monsoon; Sea level rise etc.
evidenceBullets: Arctic Amplification is the phenomenon where the Arctic warms significantly faster than the rest of the Earth.
Reduced albedo from melting reflective ice acts as a feedback loop driving Arctic Amplification.
Lapse rate feedback in the Arctic causes greenhouse gas warming to be most pronounced near the surface, unlike in the tropics.
The Water Vapour Triple Effect contributes to amplification by creating more cloud cover and acting as a greenhouse gas.
Arctic Amplification impacts India by disrupting the Indian Monsoon and contributing to sea level rise.
